questions.question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE while computing \(First\) and \(Follow\) during top-down parsing by a compiler?

(GATE CSE 2025 SET-1)
options:
A.

For a production \(A \to \epsilon , \epsilon \) will be added to \(First(A) \).

B.

If there is any input right end marker, it will be added to\( First(S) \), where \( S \) is the start symbol.

C.

For a production \( A \to \epsilon, \epsilon \) will be added to \( Follow(A) \).

D.

If there is any input right end marker, it will be added to \( Follow(S) \), where \( S \) is the start symbol.
correct_answer:
A.

For a production \(A \to \epsilon , \epsilon \) will be added to \(First(A) \).

D.

If there is any input right end marker, it will be added to \( Follow(S) \), where \( S \) is the start symbol.
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Consider the following grammar \(G \), with \(S \) as the start symbol. The grammar \(G \) has three incomplete productions denoted by (1), (2), and (3).

\( \begin{aligned} & S \rightarrow d a T \mid \quad(1) \\ & T \rightarrow a S|b T|(2) \\ & R \rightarrow \text { (3) } \mid \epsilon \end{aligned} \)

The set of terminals is \(\{a, b, c, d, f\} \). The FIRST and FOLLOW sets of the different non-terminals are as follows.

\( \begin{aligned} & \operatorname{FIRST}(S)=\{c, d, f\}, \operatorname{FIRST}(T)=\{a, b, \epsilon\}, \operatorname{FIRST}(R)=\{c, \epsilon\} \\ & \operatorname{FOLLOW}(S)=\operatorname{FOLLOW}(T)=\{c, f, \$\}, \operatorname{FOLLOW}(R)=\{f\} \end{aligned} \)

Which one of the following options CORRECTLY fills in the incomplete productions?

(GATE CSE 2024 SET-1)
options:
C.

(1) \(S \rightarrow f R \) (2) \(T \rightarrow c T \) (3) \(R \rightarrow c R \)

D.

(1) \(S \rightarrow R f \) (2) \(T \rightarrow c T \) (3) \(R \rightarrow c R \)

questions.question:
Consider the following grammar along with translation rules.
\( \begin{aligned} &S \rightarrow S_1 \# T & & \{S._{val}=S_{1.val}*T._{val} \} \\ &S \rightarrow T & & \{S._{val}=T._{val} \} \\ &T \rightarrow T_1 \% R & & \{T._{val}=T_{1.val} \div R._{val} \} \\ &T \rightarrow R & & \{T._{val}=R._{val} \} \\ &R \rightarrow id & & \{R._{val}=id._{val} \} \\ \end{aligned}\)
Here \( \#\) and \( \%\) are operators and \(id\) is a token that represents an integer and \(id_{.val}\) represents the corresponding integer value. The set of non-terminals is \( \{S,T,R,P \}\) and a subscripted non-terminal indicates an instance of the non-terminal.
Using this translation scheme, the computed value of \(S_{.val}\) for root of the parse tree for the expression \(20 \# 10 \% 5 \# 8 \% 2 \% 2\) is

C.

Data flow analysis is necessary for run-time memory management.

D.

D.

LR(1) parsing is sufficient for deterministic context-free languages.

\(S\rightarrow aAbB|bAaB|\varepsilon\)
\(A\rightarrow S\)
\(B\rightarrow S\)

(GATE CSE 2012)
options:
A.

E1: S\(\rightarrow\)aAbB, A\(\rightarrow\)S
E2: S\(\rightarrow\)bAaB, B\(\rightarrow\)S
E3: B\(\rightarrow\)S

B.

E1: S\(\rightarrow\) aAbB, S \(\rightarrow \varepsilon \)

E3: B\(\rightarrow \varepsilon\)S

C.

E1: S \(\rightarrow\) aAbB, S \(\rightarrow \varepsilon\)

E3: B \(\rightarrow\) S

E3: B\(\rightarrow\)S

questions.question:
The grammar whose productions are
\(\langle\text{stmt}\rangle \to\text{ if id then } \langle\text{stmt}\rangle\)
\(\langle\text{stmt}\rangle\to\text{ if id then } \langle\text{stmt}\rangle\text{ else } \langle \text{stmt}\rangle\)
\(\langle\text{stmt}\rangle \to\text{ id }:=\text{ id}\)
is ambiguous because

(GATE CSE 1996)
options:
A.

the sentence
\(\text{ if a then if b then c:= d } \)
has more than two parse trees

B.

the left most and right most derivations of the sentence
\(\text{ if a then if b then c:= d } \)
give rise to different parse trees

C.

the sentence
\(\text{ if a then if b then c:= d else c:= f} \)
has more than two parse trees

D.

the sentence
\(\text{ if a then if b then c:= d else c:= f} \)
has two parse trees
questions.correct_answer:
D.

the sentence
\(\text{ if a then if b then c:= d else c:= f} \)
has two parse trees
